title: Analyzing Alito's Leaked Draft, How Roe Died, and Where We Go From Here
author: James Silberman
orientation: Silberman analyzes the leaked Alito draft of the Dobbs majority opinion, recapping its historical and stare decisis arguments against Roe while criticizing it for leaving abortion to popular vote rather than recognizing 14th Amendment personhood, and credits the abolitionist movement's confrontational posture for the political momentum that finally moved the Court.
audiences: Abolitionists evaluating the Dobbs draft on principle
Constitutional law readers tracking the legal reasoning
State legislators and activists planning post-Roe strategy
Movement strategists comparing pro-life and abolitionist tactics

questions.question: What does the leaked draft actually say about the fate of Roe and Casey?
questions.audience: Constitutional law readers tracking the legal reasoning
questions.answer: Politico published a 98-page draft majority opinion in Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health authored by Justice Alito in which he writes for the conservative bloc that the two precedents protecting legal abortion must be discarded.
questions.quote: In the 98-page document, Alito writes for the majority (which presumably is made up of Alito and the other four conservative justices), “We hold that *Roe* and *Casey* must be overruled.”

questions.question: Should we treat the leak as proof Roe is gone?
questions.answer: No. Justices vote privately months ahead of the announcement and can still flip, as Roberts famously did on the Obamacare penalty; the leak is a political pressure tactic and nothing is final until the decision drops.
questions.quote: The draft does not mean that *Roe* is necessarily going to be overturned. The justices vote privately among each other months before the announcement of the final decision in every case, and can change their vote during that time.

questions.question: How does Alito dismantle the historical claim at the heart of Roe?
questions.audience: Constitutional law readers tracking the legal reasoning
questions.answer: He shows that no state constitution, federal court, state court, or scholarly treatise recognized a constitutional right to abortion before the late 20th century, contradicting Roe's assertion that such a right is deeply rooted in American history and tradition.
questions.quote: “Until the latter part of the 20th century, there was no support in American law for a constitutional right to obtain an abortion. Zero. None. No state constitutional provision had recognized such a right. Until a few years before *Roe* was handed down, no federal or state court had recognized such a right. Nor had any scholarly treatise of which we are aware…

questions.question: What was the legal status of abortion when the 14th Amendment was adopted?
questions.audience: Constitutional law readers tracking the legal reasoning
questions.answer: Three-quarters of states had criminalized abortion at every stage of pregnancy by the time of ratification, with the rest moving the same direction, undermining the claim that legal abortion is part of the nation's tradition.
questions.quote: By the time of the adoption of the Fourteenth Amendment, three-quarters of the States had made abortion a crime at any stage of pregnancy, and the remaining states would soon follow.

questions.question: Why does Alito conclude that stare decisis cannot save Roe?
questions.audience: Constitutional law readers tracking the legal reasoning
questions.answer: He weighs five factors—the nature of the error, the quality of the reasoning, the workability of the imposed rules, the disruption to other areas of law, and the absence of concrete reliance—and finds all five cut decisively against keeping Roe and Casey.
questions.quote: “five factors weigh strongly in favor of overruling *Roe* and *Casey*: the nature of their error, the quality of their reasoning, the ‘workability’ of the rules they imposed on the country, their disruptive effect on other areas of the law, and the absence of concrete reliance.”

questions.question: How did Roe corrode the rest of constitutional law?
questions.audience: Constitutional law readers tracking the legal reasoning
questions.answer: Alito quotes Justice O'Connor to make the point that the Court routinely abandoned settled doctrines whenever they got in the way of protecting abortion, distorting jurisprudence well beyond the abortion context.
questions.quote: “no legal rule or doctrine is safe from ad hoc nullification by the Court when an occasion for its application arises in a case involving state regulation of abortion.”

questions.question: Why do abolitionists refuse to celebrate the draft as a win for justice?
questions.audience: Abolitionists evaluating the Dobbs draft on principle
questions.answer: Because Alito hands the abortion question back to popular vote rather than recognizing preborn children as persons under the 14th Amendment, perpetuating a dehumanizing legal theory that will still leave many children to die.
questions.quote: While various positive impacts may result from this decision, Alito perpetuated an absurd and dehumanizing legal theory that will result in the death of many children.

questions.question: What is the abolitionist 14th Amendment argument Alito ignored?
questions.audience: Abolitionists evaluating the Dobbs draft on principle
questions.answer: Drawing on Josh Craddock's scholarship, Silberman argues that "person" in the Constitution has historically covered all human beings, so the Equal Protection Clause itself forbids abortion regardless of state legislative preferences.
questions.quote: As such, the 14th Amendment’s guarantee that “no state shall…deny to any person within its jurisdiction the equal protection of the laws” is a prohibition on abortion.

questions.question: What is wrong with leaving abortion "to the people"?
questions.audience: Abolitionists evaluating the Dobbs draft on principle
questions.answer: If majorities can decide which human beings count as persons, then the 14th Amendment protects no one; any class of human beings can be stripped of equal protection by being defined out of personhood.
questions.quote: But if the people are at liberty to pick and choose which human beings count as persons, then the 14th Amendment doesn’t protect anyone. Under this theory, any group can be denied the equal protection of the law provided the people define them as non-persons.

questions.question: Why didn't the 1992 Casey court overturn Roe when conditions seemed perfect?
questions.audience: Movement strategists comparing pro-life and abolitionist tactics
questions.answer: Despite an 8-1 Republican-appointed majority, the Court reaffirmed Roe largely because the pro-life movement was politically weak, treating crumbs as victories and giving the justices no cost for compromise.
questions.quote: Bauer’s disposition is emblematic of the pro-life leaders since *Roe*. They come begging to the courts every few years, leave with scraps, and pretend they’re winning.

questions.question: What changed in the political landscape that Silberman credits for moving the justices this time?
questions.audience: Movement strategists comparing pro-life and abolitionist tactics
questions.answer: The Abolitionist Movement made confrontational demands—state nullification bills, denominational resolutions to ignore Roe, amicus briefs calling the Court to repent—shifting culture and politics in ways the more moderate justices could feel.
questions.quote: The culture and politics at the state level in many areas were shifting toward abortion’s abolition and *Roe*‘s nullification, a fact which undoubtedly impacted the justices, especially the more moderate ones.

questions.question: What posture does Silberman prescribe for the next phase of the fight?
questions.audience: Movement strategists comparing pro-life and abolitionist tactics
questions.answer: Quoting Frederick Douglass, he argues that power only yields to demands; activists must be confrontational rather than deferential if they want to drive cultural and political change.
questions.quote: If you want a tyrant to relent, threaten their authority. Be confrontational. Make demands. This is the posture that brings cultural and political change.

questions.question: Even if Roe falls, what work remains in the states?
questions.audience: State legislators and activists planning post-Roe strategy
questions.answer: No state will yet have made preborn children equal under the law, so legislatures must still be persuaded to enact true equal protection rather than half-measures that punish providers but not perpetrators.
questions.quote: we still have to persuade state legislatures to [make preborn children equal under law](https://freethestates.org/criminalization/index.html), which no state will have done, even if *Roe* is overturned.

questions.question: Why does Silberman point to Oklahoma's SB612 as a cautionary tale?
questions.audience: State legislators and activists planning post-Roe strategy
questions.answer: The much-celebrated Oklahoma bill criminalizes abortionists but explicitly exempts the mother who hires them, leaving the killing of preborn children legally permissible from her side and falling short of equal protection.
questions.quote: Oklahoma’s recently passed, [much-hyped bill](https://freethestates.org/2022/04/oklahomas-latest-abortion-ban-sb612-isnt-worth-the-paper-its-printed-on/index.html) makes it illegal for abortionists to perform abortions but allows the mother to murder her children with impunity.

related.title: 4 Abolitionist Rapid Reactions to the (Wrongly Decided) Dobbs Decision
related.reason: Silberman's follow-up after the actual Dobbs ruling, applying the same abolitionist critique he previewed here.

related.title: SCOTUS Arguments in Dobbs: Three Abolitionist Takeaways and One Prediction
related.reason: Earlier abolitionist read of the same Dobbs case during oral arguments, useful for tracking how the analysis evolved.

related.title: Abolitionist Lawyer to File Amicus Brief in SCOTUS Abortion Case
related.reason: Documents the equal-protection amicus brief Silberman alludes to when describing abolitionist demands made on the Court.

related.title: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
related.reason: Earlier Silberman piece building on Josh Craddock's scholarship, the same source this article cites for the 14th Amendment personhood argument.

related.title: Oklahoma's Latest "Abortion Ban" (SB612) Isn't Worth the Paper It's Printed On
related.reason: The full critique of the Oklahoma bill that Silberman invokes as a warning about post-Roe legislative half-measures.

related.title: Nullification
related.reason: Reference page for the state-level Roe nullification strategy Silberman credits with shifting the political ground under the Court.

related.title: Refuting Dana Hall McCain's Nonsensical Anti-Abolitionist Slander
related.reason: Defends the equal-protection abolitionist case against pro-life critics, fleshing out the personhood argument this article summarizes.
